Spatio-temporal Modelling, Lab 5
2023-11-22

This exercise will be discussed on 2023-11-29.

1 Spatial modelling: introductory matter

1.1 meuse

The meuse data set is a data set collected from the Meuse floodplain. The heavy metals analyzed are copper, cadmium, lead and zinc concentrations, extracted from the topsoil of a part of the Meuse floodplain, the area around Meers and Maasband (Limburg, The Netherlands). The data were collected during fieldwork in 1990 (Rikken and Rijn 1993; Burrough and McDonnell 1998). Let´s have a look at the data. If not done yet, download the data set from learnweb. The code below assumes the data to sit in subdirectory data of your working directory. Adapt the path to wherever you have stored the data on your disk.

library(gstat)
Warning: Paket 'gstat' wurde unter R Version 4.3.1 erstellt
library(sp)
library(rgdal)
Warning: Paket 'rgdal' wurde unter R Version 4.3.1 erstellt
Please note that rgdal will be retired during October 2023,
plan transition to sf/stars/terra functions using GDAL and PROJ
at your earliest convenience.
See https://r-spatial.org/r/2023/05/15/evolution4.html and https://github.com/r-spatial/evolution
rgdal: version: 1.6-7, (SVN revision 1203)
Geospatial Data Abstraction Library extensions to R successfully loaded
Loaded GDAL runtime: GDAL 3.6.2, released 2023/01/02
Path to GDAL shared files: C:/Users/Chro/AppData/Local/R/win-library/4.3/rgdal/gdal
 GDAL does not use iconv for recoding strings.
GDAL binary built with GEOS: TRUE 
Loaded PROJ runtime: Rel. 9.2.0, March 1st, 2023, [PJ_VERSION: 920]
Path to PROJ shared files: C:/Users/Chro/AppData/Local/R/win-library/4.3/rgdal/proj
PROJ CDN enabled: FALSE
Linking to sp version:2.0-0
To mute warnings of possible GDAL/OSR exportToProj4() degradation,
use options("rgdal_show_exportToProj4_warnings"="none") before loading sp or rgdal.
meuse <- readOGR("./data/meuse.gpkg")

with 155 features

summary(meuse)

objects()
[1] "has_annotations" "meuse"

1.2 Models: the formula interface; linear regression

Most statisticians (and many earth scientists as well) like to analyse data through models: models reflect the hypotheses we entertain, and from fitting models, analysing model output, and analysing graphs of fitted values and residuals we can learn from the data whether a given hypothesis was reasonable or not.

Many models are variations of regression models. Here, we will illustrate an example of a simple linear regression model involving the heavy metal pollution data in meuse. We will entertain the hypothesis that zinc concentration, present in variable zinc, depends linearly on distance to the river, present in dist. We can express dependency of y on x by a formula, coded as y~x. Formulas can have multiple right-hand sides for multiple linear regression, as y~x1+x2; they also can contain expressions (transformations) of variables, as in log(y)~sqrt(x). See ?formula for a full description of the full functionality.

To calculate a linear regression model for the meuse data:

zinc.lm = lm(zinc ~ dist, meuse)
class(zinc.lm)
[1] "lm"
summary(zinc.lm)

Call:
lm(formula = zinc ~ dist, data = meuse)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-475.20 -189.94  -52.94  120.15 1088.80 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)   756.70      35.66   21.22   <2e-16 ***
dist        -1195.67     114.84  -10.41   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 281.7 on 153 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.4147,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.4109 
F-statistic: 108.4 on 1 and 153 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Do the following yourself:

par(mfrow=c(1,2)) #to get two plots in one row
plot(zinc.lm)

Besides the variety of plots you now obtain, there are many further custom options you can set that can help analysing these data. When you ask help by ?plot, it does not provide very helpful information. To get help on the plot method that is called when plotting an object of class lm, remember that the function called is plot.lm. Read the help of plot.lm by ?plot.lm . You can customize the plot call, e.g. by

par(mfrow=c(1,2)) 
plot(zinc.lm, add.smooth = FALSE)

In this call, the name of the second function argument is added because the argument panel is in a very rear position. Adding the FALSE as a positional argument, as in

par(mfrow=c(1,2)) 
 plot(zinc.lm, , , , , , , FALSE)

will not work as it comes after the ... argument, which may contain an arbitrary number of arguments that are passed to underlying plot routines. But the following two commands (search for the difference!) both work:

 par(mfrow=c(1,2)) 
plot(zinc.lm, caption = c("Residuen ~ Modellierte Werte", "QQ-Plot mit Normalverteilung", 
    " Standardisierte Residuen ~ Modellierte Werte", "Cook's Distanz", "Residuen vs Leverage", 
    "Cook's Distanz vs Leverage"))

par(mfrow=c(1,2)) 
plot(zinc.lm, , c("Residuen ~ Modellierte Werte", "QQ-Plot mit Normalverteilung", 
    " Standardisierte Residuen ~ Modellierte Werte", "Cook's Distanz", "Residuen vs Leverage", 
    "Cook's Distanz vs Leverage"))

Exercise 1

Exercise 1

For this regression model, which of the following violated assumptions underlying linear regression are true (if any):

  1. none
  2. residuals are heteroscedastic (i.e., their variance is not constant over the range of fitted values)
  3. residuals are not normally distributed (their distribution is for example non-symmetric)
  4. residuals are heteroscedastic and not normally distributed

and explain how you can tell that this is the case.

The example above is a spatial model only to the extent that the explanatory variable dist in this case contains some spatial information (distance to the river). We are not using the spatial information contained in the SpatialPointsDataFrame with respect to the location (coordinates) of the heavy metal measurements. In the following, we will look at actual spatial data analysis starting with how to handle spatial data in R.

1.3 Spatial data in R (I): the sp package

The R package sp (Pebesma and Bivand 2022a) provides classes and methods for spatial data; currently it can deal with points, grids, lines and polygons. It further provides interfaces to various GIS functions such as overlay, projection and reading and writing of external GIS formats.

1.3.1 Points

Try:

class(meuse)
[1] "SpatialPointsDataFrame"
attr(,"package")
[1] "sp"
summary(meuse)
Object of class SpatialPointsDataFrame
Coordinates:
             min    max
coords.x1 178605 181390
coords.x2 329714 333611
Is projected: TRUE 
proj4string :
[+proj=sterea +lat_0=52.1561605555556 +lon_0=5.38763888888889
+k=0.9999079 +x_0=155000 +y_0=463000 +ellps=bessel +units=m +no_defs]
Number of points: 155
Data attributes:
    cadmium           copper            lead            zinc       
 Min.   : 0.200   Min.   : 14.00   Min.   : 37.0   Min.   : 113.0  
 1st Qu.: 0.800   1st Qu.: 23.00   1st Qu.: 72.5   1st Qu.: 198.0  
 Median : 2.100   Median : 31.00   Median :123.0   Median : 326.0  
 Mean   : 3.246   Mean   : 40.32   Mean   :153.4   Mean   : 469.7  
 3rd Qu.: 3.850   3rd Qu.: 49.50   3rd Qu.:207.0   3rd Qu.: 674.5  
 Max.   :18.100   Max.   :128.00   Max.   :654.0   Max.   :1839.0  
                                                                   
      elev             dist               om            ffreq          
 Min.   : 5.180   Min.   :0.00000   Min.   : 1.000   Length:155        
 1st Qu.: 7.546   1st Qu.:0.07569   1st Qu.: 5.300   Class :character  
 Median : 8.180   Median :0.21184   Median : 6.900   Mode  :character  
 Mean   : 8.165   Mean   :0.24002   Mean   : 7.478                     
 3rd Qu.: 8.955   3rd Qu.:0.36407   3rd Qu.: 9.000                     
 Max.   :10.520   Max.   :0.88039   Max.   :17.000                     
                                    NA's   :2                          
     soil               lime             landuse              dist.m      
 Length:155         Length:155         Length:155         Min.   :  10.0  
 Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   1st Qu.:  80.0  
 Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Median : 270.0  
                                                          Mean   : 290.3  
                                                          3rd Qu.: 450.0  
                                                          Max.   :1000.0  
                                                                          
      id           
 Length:155        
 Class :character  
 Mode  :character  
                   
                   
                   
                   
sp.theme(TRUE)
spplot(meuse, "zinc", key.space = "right")

The plot now obtained shows points, but without reference where on earth they lie. You could e.g. add a reference by showing the coordinate system:

spplot(meuse, "zinc", key.space = "right", scales = list(draw = TRUE))

but this tells little about what we see. As another option, you can add geographic reference by e.g. lines of rivers, or administrative boundaries. In our example, we prepared a polygon of the Meuse river boundaries, and will add it as reference:

meuse.riv <- readOGR("./data/meuse_river.gpkg")
OGR data source with driver: GPKG 
Source: "C:\Users\Chro\Files\teaching\mstp_2223(contains_git_repo)\mstp\labs\data\meuse_river.gpkg", layer: "meuse_river"
with 1 features
It has 0 fields
meuse.lt = list("sp.polygons", meuse.riv, fill = "grey")
spplot(meuse, "zinc", key.space = "right", sp.layout = meuse.lt)

The sp.layout argument contains map elements to be added to an spplot plot.

Note that the points in the plot partly fall in the river; this may be attributed to one or more of the following reasons: (i) the river coordinates are not correct, (ii) the point coordinates are not correct, (iii) points were taken on the river bank when the water was low, whereas the river boundary indicates the high water extent of the river.

1.3.2 Grids

Try:

library(rgdal)
meuse.grid <- as(readGDAL("./data/meuse_grid.tif"),"SpatialPixelsDataFrame")
./data/meuse_grid.tif has GDAL driver GTiff 
and has 104 rows and 78 columns
class(meuse.grid)
[1] "SpatialPixelsDataFrame"
attr(,"package")
[1] "sp"

As you can see, the GeoTiff has been loaded as a SpatialPixelsDataFrame. The GeoTiff contains 5 bands: bands 1 and 2 simply indicate, whether a pixel belongs to “part.a” or “part.b” of the study area (“yes” or “no”, i.e., 1 or 0 in the corresponding band). This is an arbitrary division of the area in two parts. Band 3 shows the distance to the river Meuse, band 4 has the soil type, and band 5 indicates the number of times a pixel has been flooded before. To represent this in our SpatialPixelsDataFrame, we can assign corresponding names to the data attributes. We will also change the type of the attributes containing categorical data to factor.

names(meuse.grid) <- c("part.a","part.b","dist","soil","ffreq")
meuse.grid$soil <- as.factor(meuse.grid$soil)
meuse.grid$ffreq <- as.factor(meuse.grid$ffreq)
summary(meuse.grid)
Object of class SpatialPixelsDataFrame
Coordinates:
     min    max
x 178440 181560
y 329600 333760
Is projected: TRUE 
proj4string :
[+proj=sterea +lat_0=52.1561605555556 +lon_0=5.38763888888889
+k=0.9999079 +x_0=155000 +y_0=463000 +ellps=bessel +units=m +no_defs]
Number of points: 3103
Grid attributes:
  cellcentre.offset cellsize cells.dim
x            178460       40        78
y            329620       40       104
Data attributes:
     part.a           part.b            dist        soil     ffreq   
 Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :0.0000   1:1665   1: 779  
 1st Qu.:0.0000   1st Qu.:0.0000   1st Qu.:0.1193   2:1084   2:1335  
 Median :0.0000   Median :1.0000   Median :0.2715   3: 354   3: 989  
 Mean   :0.3986   Mean   :0.6014   Mean   :0.2971                    
 3rd Qu.:1.0000   3rd Qu.:1.0000   3rd Qu.:0.4402                    
 Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :0.9926

Let´s try to plot the data:

meuse.lt = list(riv = list("sp.polygons", meuse.riv, fill = "grey"), pts = list("sp.points", 
    meuse, pch = 3, cex = 0.5, col = "black"))
spplot(meuse.grid, sp.layout = meuse.lt, key.space = "right")

For explanation on the sp.layout argument, read ?spplot; much of the codes in them (pch, cex, col) are documented in ?par.

Note that spplot plots points on top of the grid, and the grid cells on top of the polygon with the river. (When printing to pdf, transparency options can be used to combine both.)

1.4 Import/export: rgdal

R package rgdal provides several drivers for import/export of spatial data. We have used it above to read the vector and raster data meuse, meuse.riv and meuse.grid. An overview of available drivers for vector data (points, lines, polygons) can be obtained by

ogrDrivers()
             name                                            long_name write
1      AmigoCloud                                           AmigoCloud  TRUE
2          AVCBin                             Arc/Info Binary Coverage FALSE
3          AVCE00                        Arc/Info E00 (ASCII) Coverage FALSE
4             BAG                           Bathymetry Attributed Grid  TRUE
5             CAD                                       AutoCAD Driver FALSE
6           Carto                                                Carto  TRUE
7             CSV                         Comma Separated Value (.csv)  TRUE
8             CSW               OGC CSW (Catalog  Service for the Web) FALSE
9             DGN                                     Microstation DGN  TRUE
10            DXF                                          AutoCAD DXF  TRUE
11         EDIGEO                        French EDIGEO exchange format FALSE
12           EEDA                                Earth Engine Data API FALSE
13  Elasticsearch                                       Elastic Search  TRUE
14 ESRI Shapefile                                       ESRI Shapefile  TRUE
15       ESRIJSON                                             ESRIJSON FALSE
16     FlatGeobuf                                           FlatGeobuf  TRUE
17     Geoconcept                                           Geoconcept  TRUE
18        GeoJSON                                              GeoJSON  TRUE
19     GeoJSONSeq                                     GeoJSON Sequence  TRUE
20         GeoRSS                                               GeoRSS  TRUE
21            GML                      Geography Markup Language (GML)  TRUE
22           GPKG                                           GeoPackage  TRUE
23       GPSBabel                                             GPSBabel  TRUE
24            GPX                                                  GPX  TRUE
25           HTTP                                HTTP Fetching Wrapper FALSE
26         Idrisi                                 Idrisi Vector (.vct) FALSE
27            JML                                         OpenJUMP JML  TRUE
28    JP2OpenJPEG           JPEG-2000 driver based on OpenJPEG library FALSE
29            KML                        Keyhole Markup Language (KML)  TRUE

31   MapInfo File                                         MapInfo File  TRUE

87              NDF                                        NLAPS Data Format
88           netCDF                               Network Common Data Format
89         NGSGEOID                              NOAA NGS Geoid Height Grids

The full set of theoretically available drivers with documentation are given here and here.

Export the data set to KML

 library(rgdal)
 data(meuse)
 coordinates(meuse) = ~x+y
 proj4string(meuse) = CRS("+init=epsg:28992")
 proj4string(meuse)
 meuse.ll = spTransform(meuse, CRS("+proj=longlat"))
 writeOGR(meuse.ll, "meuse.kml", "meuse.kml", driver="KML")

and import it in google earth.

1.5 Spatial data in R (II): the packages sf and stars

The package sf (Pebesma 2022a), which first appeared on CRAN in 2016, is intended to replace the vector parts of sp (among others). It was developed “to move spatial data analysis in R closer to standards-based approaches seen in the industry and open source projects, to build upon more modern versions of the open source geospatial software stack […], and to allow for integration of R spatial software with the tidyverse […]” (Pebesma and Bivand 2022b, chap. 7.1).
The package stars (Pebesma 2022b), which first came to CRAN in 2018, was developed for analyzing raster (and vector) data cubes. It is meant to extend current packages for raster data with functionality to represent dynamic raster stacks (e.g. time series) and to scale computations to data beyond local disc sizes (Pebesma and Bivand 2022b, chap. 7.4).

Both packages are developed to work closely together and can be used for all spatial analyses throughout our exercises. In the following, we show how to repeat the examples from above for handling points and grids with sf and stars.

1.5.1 Points

library(sf)
Linking to GEOS 3.11.1, GDAL 3.6.2, PROJ 9.1.1; sf_use_s2() is TRUE
meuse.sf <- st_read("./data/meuse.gpkg")
Reading layer `meuse' from data source 
  `C:\Users\Chro\Files\teaching\mstp_2223(contains_git_repo)\mstp\labs\data\meuse.gpkg' 
  using driver `GPKG'
Simple feature collection with 155 features and 13 fields
Geometry type: POINT
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: 178605 ymin: 329714 xmax: 181390 ymax: 333611
Projected CRS: Amersfoort / RD New
class(meuse.sf)
[1] "sf"         "data.frame"
summary(meuse.sf)
    cadmium           copper            lead            zinc       
 Min.   : 0.200   Min.   : 14.00   Min.   : 37.0   Min.   : 113.0  
 1st Qu.: 0.800   1st Qu.: 23.00   1st Qu.: 72.5   1st Qu.: 198.0  
 Median : 2.100   Median : 31.00   Median :123.0   Median : 326.0  
 Mean   : 3.246   Mean   : 40.32   Mean   :153.4   Mean   : 469.7  
 3rd Qu.: 3.850   3rd Qu.: 49.50   3rd Qu.:207.0   3rd Qu.: 674.5  
 Max.   :18.100   Max.   :128.00   Max.   :654.0   Max.   :1839.0  
                                                                   
      elev             dist               om            ffreq          
 Min.   : 5.180   Min.   :0.00000   Min.   : 1.000   Length:155        
 1st Qu.: 7.546   1st Qu.:0.07569   1st Qu.: 5.300   Class :character  
 Median : 8.180   Median :0.21184   Median : 6.900   Mode  :character  
 Mean   : 8.165   Mean   :0.24002   Mean   : 7.478                     
 3rd Qu.: 8.955   3rd Qu.:0.36407   3rd Qu.: 9.000                     
 Max.   :10.520   Max.   :0.88039   Max.   :17.000                     
                                    NA's   :2                          
     soil               lime             landuse              dist.m      
 Length:155         Length:155         Length:155         Min.   :  10.0  
 Class :character   Class :character   Class :character   1st Qu.:  80.0  
 Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Mode  :character   Median : 270.0  
                                                          Mean   : 290.3  
                                                          3rd Qu.: 450.0  
                                                          Max.   :1000.0  
                                                                          
      id                       geom    
 Length:155         POINT        :155  
 Class :character   epsg:NA      :  0  
 Mode  :character   +proj=ster...:  0  
                                       
                                       
                                       
                                       
plot(meuse.sf)
Warning: plotting the first 10 out of 13 attributes; use max.plot = 13 to plot
all

plot(meuse.sf["zinc"],axes=T)

Again, we can add the meuse river as refeference.

meuse.riv.sf <- st_read("./data/meuse_river.gpkg")
Reading layer `meuse_river' from data source 
  `C:\Users\Chro\Files\teaching\mstp_2223(contains_git_repo)\mstp\labs\data\meuse_river.gpkg' 
  using driver `GPKG'
Simple feature collection with 1 feature and 0 fields
Geometry type: POLYGON
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: 178304 ymin: 325698.5 xmax: 182331.5 ymax: 337684.8
Projected CRS: Amersfoort / RD New
plot(meuse.sf["zinc"],axes=T,reset=F)
plot(meuse.riv.sf,col='grey',add=T)

1.5.2 Grids

Try:

library(stars)
Warning: Paket 'stars' wurde unter R Version 4.3.1 erstellt
Lade nötiges Paket: abind
meuse.grid.stars <- read_stars("./data/meuse_grid.tif")
meuse.grid.stars
stars object with 3 dimensions and 1 attribute
attribute(s):
                Min.   1st Qu. Median      Mean 3rd Qu. Max.  NA's
meuse_grid.tif     0 0.1192915      1 0.9884603       1    3 25045
dimension(s):
     from  to offset delta              refsys point x/y
x       1  78 178440    40 Amersfoort / RD New FALSE [x]
y       1 104 333760   -40 Amersfoort / RD New FALSE [y]
band    1   5     NA    NA                  NA    NA

The bands of our data set are automatically interpreted as another dimension of the data (here you can recognize the characteristic of stars to naturally support multidimensional raster data). However, in this case our bands do not represent another dimension (like for example having the same measurements at different times in the different bands), so instead we want them to be interpreted as 5 different attributes of a 2-dimensional array. This can be done using the function split:

meuse.grid.stars <- split(meuse.grid.stars, "band")
meuse.grid.stars
stars object with 2 dimensions and 5 attributes
attribute(s):
    Min.   1st Qu.   Median      Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. NA's
X1     0 0.0000000 0.000000 0.3986465 1.000000 1.000000 5009
X2     0 0.0000000 1.000000 0.6013535 1.000000 1.000000 5009
X3     0 0.1192915 0.271535 0.2971195 0.440159 0.992607 5009
X4     1 1.0000000 1.000000 1.5775056 2.000000 3.000000 5009
X5     1 1.0000000 2.000000 2.0676764 3.000000 3.000000 5009
dimension(s):
  from  to offset delta              refsys point x/y
x    1  78 178440    40 Amersfoort / RD New FALSE [x]
y    1 104 333760   -40 Amersfoort / RD New FALSE [y]

Similar to above, we can now assign the names to the attributes and change the type of the attributes representing categorical variables to factor.

names(meuse.grid.stars) <- c("part.a","part.b","dist","soil","ffreq")
meuse.grid.stars$soil <- as.factor(meuse.grid.stars$soil)
meuse.grid.stars$ffreq <- as.factor(meuse.grid.stars$ffreq)
meuse.grid.stars
stars object with 2 dimensions and 5 attributes
attribute(s):
    part.a          part.b           dist         soil        ffreq     
 Min.   :0.000   Min.   :0.000   Min.   :0.000   1   :1665   1   : 779  
 1st Qu.:0.000   1st Qu.:0.000   1st Qu.:0.119   2   :1084   2   :1335  
 Median :0.000   Median :1.000   Median :0.272   3   : 354   3   : 989  
 Mean   :0.399   Mean   :0.601   Mean   :0.297   NA's:5009   NA's:5009  
 3rd Qu.:1.000   3rd Qu.:1.000   3rd Qu.:0.440                          
 Max.   :1.000   Max.   :1.000   Max.   :0.993                          
 NA's   :5009    NA's   :5009    NA's   :5009                           
dimension(s):
  from  to offset delta              refsys point x/y
x    1  78 178440    40 Amersfoort / RD New FALSE [x]
y    1 104 333760   -40 Amersfoort / RD New FALSE [y]

Let´s try a simple plot before looking more into visualization of sf and starsobjects.

plot(meuse.grid.stars[3], col=heat.colors(10))

1.6 Visualizing spatial data with the tmap package

In the previous section, we have used the function spplot() to create plots from data handled by the package sp, such as SpatialPointsDataFrame or SpatialPixelsDataFrame. While there are base plot methods for sf and stars objects as well (we have used some of them in the examples above, more examples can be found in the lecture material), we will use the package tmap (Tennekes 2022) for plotting objects of those classes throughout this exercise. tmap allows easy handling of sf and stars objects combined in one plot and offers a lot of utility for creating and styling maps and other visualizations of spatial data. The syntax for plotting is very simple and straightforward: the basic building blog of a plot is a tm_shape element, which defines the spatial data object (raster or vector) to be added to the map plus one or more layer elements, e.g., tm_raster or tm_polygons, which define how the object is to be plotted (and which aspects of it). Spatial data objects can be, e.g., an sf or stars object, but also objects from the packages sp or raster. All these elements are combined via the + operator. The example below creates a plot with one layer showing the “dist” attribute of meuse.grid.stars together with the polygon of the Meuse river.

library(tmap)
Warning: Paket 'tmap' wurde unter R Version 4.3.2 erstellt
Breaking News: tmap 3.x is retiring. Please test v4, e.g. with
remotes::install_github('r-tmap/tmap')
tm_shape(meuse.grid.stars)+tm_raster("dist",n=10)+tm_shape(meuse.riv.sf)+tm_polygons() #plots the `dist` attribute of the spatial data object `meuse.grid.stars` and the polygon of the Meuse river contained in spatial data object `meuse.riv.sf`

The functions for adding the layers like tm_raster() or tm_polygons() allow for several arguments further specifying how the layers should be visualized. In the code above we only specified that the raster layer should show the “dist” attribute with a preferred number of 10 classes. Many other arguments can be set as well (see below for an example that adds the point data of the “meuse.sf” data set with symbols of a specific shape, size and color).

bpy.palette <- bpy.colors() #define color palette for the following plots equal to the one used above by spplot()
tm_shape(meuse.grid.stars) +
  tm_raster("dist",n=10, palette = bpy.palette) + 
  tm_shape(meuse.riv.sf) + 
  tm_polygons() + 
  tm_shape(meuse.sf) + 
  tm_symbols(shape = 3,size = 0.2,col="green")

There are also other tmap elements, e.g., tm_layout(), which further specifies the overall map layout, or tm_facets(), which can be used to create small multiples in a map.

tm_shape(meuse.grid.stars) + 
  tm_raster(c("dist","soil")) + 
  tm_shape(meuse.riv.sf)+ tm_polygons() + 
  tm_shape(meuse) + tm_symbols(shape = 3,size = 0.2,col="black") + 
  tm_facets() + 
  tm_layout(legend.outside.size = 0.15, main.title = "distance to river and soil type in one plot", panel.labels = c("distance to river","soil type"))

tmap can be used not only for plotting static maps, but also for visualizing spatial data in an interactive viewer. Changing the mode to “view” will cause all visualizations to be shown in an interactive leaflet map including selectable background layers as well as zoom and pan functionality. The following code shows an example (note that after creating the interactive map, we switch back to the mode “plot” for creating static maps in the following sections).

tmap_mode("view")
tmap mode set to interactive viewing
tm_shape(meuse.grid.stars) + 
  tm_raster("dist",n=10) + 
  tm_shape(meuse.riv)+tm_polygons()
tmap_mode("plot")
tmap mode set to plotting

For more information on the use of tmap please refer to the get started! vignette.

1.7 sp vs. sf & stars

Whether you prefer to use sp or sf and stars, is up to you. As mentioned above sf and stars are intended to replace sp in the long term. For now, we will always present the code for both alternatives when analyzing spatial data. You can switch between them by selecting the corresponding tabs in the code sections. You will notice that for the most part there are only minor (if any) differences between both alternatives. Most of the functions we will be using can handle objects from sp, sf and stars identically. Main differences occur when plotting the data (spplot for sp, functions from tmap for sf & stars).

2 Geostatistics

The following practical exercises cover the use of geostatistical applications in environmental research. They give an introduction to exploratory data analysis, sample variogram calculation and variogram modelling and several spatial interpolation techniques. Interpolation methods described and applied are inverse distance interpolation, trend surface fitting, thiessen polygons, ordinary (block) kriging, stratified (block) kriging and indicator (block) kriging.

The gstat R package (Pebesma and Graeler 2022) is used for all geostatistical calculations. See Pebesma (2004) for more info.

2.1 Simple interpolation algorithms

2.1.1 Trend surface analysis

Trend surface interpolation is multiple linear regression with polynomials of coordinates as independent, predictor variables. Although we could use lm to fit these models and predict them, it would require scaling of coordinates prior to prediction in most cases because higher powers of large coordinate values result in large numerical errors. The krige function in library gstat scales coordinates and computes trend surface up to order 3 (argument degree), using scaled coordinates.

  • sp
  • sf/stars
meuse.grid$tr1 = krige(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid, degree = 1)$var1.pred
[ordinary or weighted least squares prediction]
meuse.grid$tr2 = krige(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid, degree = 2)$var1.pred
[ordinary or weighted least squares prediction]
meuse.grid$tr3 = krige(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid, degree = 3)$var1.pred
[ordinary or weighted least squares prediction]
spplot(meuse.grid, c("tr1", "tr2", "tr3"), sp.layout = meuse.lt, main = "log(zinc), trend surface interpolation")

meuse.grid.stars$tr1 = krige(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse.sf, meuse.grid.stars, degree = 1)$var1.pred
[ordinary or weighted least squares prediction]
meuse.grid.stars$tr2 = krige(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse.sf, meuse.grid.stars, degree = 2)$var1.pred
[ordinary or weighted least squares prediction]
meuse.grid.stars$tr3 = krige(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse.sf, meuse.grid.stars, degree = 3)$var1.pred
[ordinary or weighted least squares prediction]
tm_shape(meuse.grid.stars) + 
  tm_raster(c("tr1","tr2","tr3"),n=20,title = "prediction", palette = bpy.palette) + 
  tm_shape(meuse.riv.sf)+tm_polygons() + 
  tm_shape(meuse)+tm_symbols(shape = 3,size = 0.2,col="black") + 
  tm_facets(free.scales = FALSE) + 
  tm_layout(legend.outside.size = 0.15, main.title = "log(zinc), trend surface interpolation", panel.labels = c("tr1","tr2","tr3"))

Note

Function surf.ls from library spatial fits trends up to order 6 with scaled coordinates.

Here is another example of the use of the ... argument in function definitions. As you can see, ?krige does not provide help on argument degree. However, it tells that any remaining arguments (...) are passed to function gstat, so look for argument degree on ?gstat to see information on this argument.

In the example above, we have fitted trends up to order 3. The higher the order, the more exactly the surface can be fitted to the data. However…

Exercise 2

Exercise 2

The danger of extreme extrapolation values is likely to occur

  1. for low trend orders
  2. for high trend orders
  3. for none of these models

Briefly explain why this is the case.

Local trends, i.e. trends in a local neighbourhood may also be fitted:

  • sp
  • sf/stars
m = krige(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid, degree=1, nmax = 10)
[ordinary or weighted least squares prediction]
spplot(m, "var1.pred", sp.layout = meuse.lt, main = "local 1st order trend")

m = krige(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse.sf, meuse.grid.stars,degree=1, nmax = 10)
[ordinary or weighted least squares prediction]
tm_shape(m) + 
  tm_raster("var1.pred", n=20, title = "prediction", palette = bpy.palette) + 
  tm_shape(meuse.riv.sf)+tm_polygons() + 
  tm_shape(meuse)+tm_symbols(shape = 3,size = 0.2,col="black") + 
  tm_layout(main.title = "local 1st order trend", legend.outside = TRUE)

2.1.2 Inverse distance interpolation

Inverse distance interpolation calculates a weighted average of points in the (by default global) neighbourhood, using weights inverse proportional to the distance of data locations to the prediction location raised to the power \(p\). This power is by default 2, but can of course be adapted:

  • sp
  • sf/stars
lzn.tp = idw(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid)
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
spplot(lzn.tp, "var1.pred", sp.layout = meuse.lt, main = "log(zinc), inverse distance interpolation")

meuse.grid$idp0.5 = idw(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid, idp = 0.5)$var1.pred
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
meuse.grid$idp02 = idw(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid, idp = 2)$var1.pred
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
meuse.grid$idp05 = idw(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid, idp = 5)$var1.pred
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
meuse.grid$idp10 = idw(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid, idp = 10)$var1.pred
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
spplot(meuse.grid, c("idp0.5", "idp02", "idp05", "idp10"), sp.layout = meuse.lt, 
    main = "log(zinc), inverse distance interpolation")

lzn.tp = idw(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse.sf, meuse.grid.stars)
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
tm_shape(m)+tm_raster("var1.pred", palette = bpy.palette, n=10) + 
  tm_shape(meuse.riv.sf) + 
  tm_polygons() + 
  tm_shape(meuse) +
  tm_symbols(shape = 3,size = 0.2,col="black") +
  tm_layout(main.title = "log(zinc), inverse distance interpolation", legend.outside = TRUE, title = "prediction" )

meuse.grid.stars$idp0.5 = idw(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid.stars, idp = 0.5)$var1.pred
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
meuse.grid.stars$idp02 = idw(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid.stars, idp = 2)$var1.pred
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
meuse.grid.stars$idp05 = idw(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid.stars, idp = 5)$var1.pred
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
meuse.grid.stars$idp10 = idw(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid.stars, idp = 10)$var1.pred
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
tm_shape(meuse.grid.stars) +
  tm_raster(c("idp0.5","idp02","idp05","idp10"),n=10, title = "prediction", palette = bpy.palette) +
  tm_shape(meuse.riv.sf) + 
  tm_polygons() + 
  tm_shape(meuse) +
  tm_symbols(shape = 3,size = 0.2,col="black") +
  tm_facets(nrow=1,free.scales = FALSE) +
  tm_layout(main.title = "log(zinc) trend surface interpolation",legend.outside.size = 0.15,panel.labels = c("idp0.5","idp02","idp05","idp10"))

Exercise 3

Exercise 3

With a larger inverse distance power (idp), the weight of the nearest data point

  1. becomes larger
  2. becomes smaller
  3. does not change

explain in words why this is the case.

2.1.3 Nearest Neighbor / Tiessen (or Voronoi) Polygons

Nearest neighbor interpolation on a regular grid (creating Thiessen Polygons) is simply obtained by using e.g. inverse distance interpolation and a neighbourhood size of one (nmax = 1). :

  • sp
  • sf/stars
lzn.tp = krige(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse, meuse.grid, nmax = 1)
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
spplot(lzn.tp, "var1.pred", sp.layout = meuse.lt, main = "Thiessen (or Voronoi) polygon interpolation of log(zinc)")

lzn.tp = krige(log(zinc) ~ 1, meuse.sf, meuse.grid.stars, nmax = 1)
[inverse distance weighted interpolation]
tm_shape(lzn.tp) + 
  tm_raster("var1.pred",n=10,title="prediction", palette = bpy.palette) +
  tm_shape(meuse.riv.sf)+tm_polygons() +
  tm_shape(meuse) +
  tm_symbols(shape = 3,size = 0.2,col="black")

3 Spatial prediction with multiple linear regression

In the data set meuse we have a large number of variables present that can be used to form predictive regression models for either of the heavy metal variables. If we want to use such a regression model for spatial prediction, we need the variables as a full spatial coverage, e.g. in the form of a regular grid covering the study area, as well.

Only few variables are available for this in meuse.grid. Let´s reload the original data set:

  • sp
  • sf/stars
meuse.grid <- as(readGDAL("./data/meuse_grid.tif"),"SpatialPixelsDataFrame") #reload original data set
./data/meuse_grid.tif has GDAL driver GTiff 
and has 104 rows and 78 columns
names(meuse.grid) <- c("part.a","part.b","dist","soil","ffreq")
meuse.grid$soil <- as.factor(meuse.grid$soil)
meuse.grid$ffreq <- as.factor(meuse.grid$ffreq)
names(meuse.grid)
[1] "part.a" "part.b" "dist"   "soil"   "ffreq" 
meuse.grid.stars <- read_stars("./data/meuse_grid.tif") #reload original data set
meuse.grid.stars <- split(meuse.grid.stars, "band")
names(meuse.grid.stars) <- c("part.a","part.b","dist","soil","ffreq")
meuse.grid.stars$soil <- as.factor(meuse.grid.stars$soil)
meuse.grid.stars$ffreq <- as.factor(meuse.grid.stars$ffreq)
names(meuse.grid.stars)
[1] "part.a" "part.b" "dist"   "soil"   "ffreq"

We already demonstrated using dist at the beginning of this exercise and we saw that some assumptions underlying linear regression were violated. Often, simple transformations of the input data can help, e.g., to overcome non-linearity or heteroscedastic residuals. Try:

  • sp
  • sf/stars
lzn.lm = lm(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse)
summary(lzn.lm)

Call:
lm(formula = log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), data = meuse)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.04624 -0.29060 -0.01869  0.26445  1.59685 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  6.99438    0.07593   92.12   <2e-16 ***
sqrt(dist)  -2.54920    0.15498  -16.45   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.4353 on 153 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6388,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6364 
F-statistic: 270.6 on 1 and 153 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
par(mfrow=c(1,2)) 
plot(lzn.lm)

plot(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse)
abline(lzn.lm)


lzn.pr = predict(lm(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse), meuse.grid, se.fit = TRUE)
meuse.grid$lzn.fit = lzn.pr$fit
spplot(meuse.grid, "lzn.fit")

meuse.grid$se.fit = lzn.pr$se.fit
spplot(meuse.grid, "se.fit")

lzn.lm = lm(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse.sf)
summary(lzn.lm)

Call:
lm(formula = log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), data = meuse.sf)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.04624 -0.29060 -0.01869  0.26445  1.59685 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  6.99438    0.07593   92.12   <2e-16 ***
sqrt(dist)  -2.54920    0.15498  -16.45   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.4353 on 153 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6388,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6364 
F-statistic: 270.6 on 1 and 153 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
par(mfrow=c(1,2)) 
plot(lzn.lm)

plot(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse.sf)
abline(lzn.lm)


lzn.pr = predict(lm(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse.sf), meuse.grid.stars, se.fit = TRUE)
meuse.grid.stars$lzn.fit = lzn.pr$fit


tm_shape(meuse.grid.stars)+tm_raster("lzn.fit",n=10, palette = bpy.palette)
meuse.grid.stars$se.fit = lzn.pr$se.fit
tm_shape(meuse.grid.stars)+tm_raster("se.fit", n=10, palette = bpy.palette)

Exercise 4

Exercise 4

Why are prediction standard errors on the log-scale largest for small and large distance to the river?

  1. Because variability is smallest for intermediate distance to river
  2. because the uncertainty in the regression slope is most prevalent when the regressor distance takes extreme values
  3. because we have too few degrees of freedom for regression (i.e., too few observations) overall
  4. because we have too little data close to the river and far away from the river

We can use the prediction errors provided by lm to calculate regression prediction intervals:

  • sp
  • sf/stars
plot(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse)
x = 0:100/100
lzn.lm = lm(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse)
pr = predict(lzn.lm, data.frame(dist = x), interval = "prediction")
abline(lzn.lm)
lines(sqrt(x), pr[, 2], lty = "dashed", col = "red")
lines(sqrt(x), pr[, 3], lty = "dashed", col = "red")
pr = predict(lzn.lm, data.frame(dist = x), interval = "confidence")
lines(sqrt(x), pr[, 2], lty = "dashed", col = "blue")
lines(sqrt(x), pr[, 3], lty = "dashed", col = "blue")
title("95% confidence intervals (blue) and prediction intervals (red)")

plot(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse.sf)
x = 0:100/100
lzn.lm = lm(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse.sf)
pr = predict(lzn.lm, data.frame(dist = x), interval = "prediction")
abline(lzn.lm)
lines(sqrt(x), pr[, 2], lty = "dashed", col = "red")
lines(sqrt(x), pr[, 3], lty = "dashed", col = "red")
pr = predict(lzn.lm, data.frame(dist = x), interval = "confidence")
lines(sqrt(x), pr[, 2], lty = "dashed", col = "blue")
lines(sqrt(x), pr[, 3], lty = "dashed", col = "blue")
title("95% confidence intervals (blue) and prediction intervals (red)")

Next, we can make maps of the 95% prediction intervals:

  • sp
  • sf/stars
lzn.lm = lm(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse)
pr = predict(lzn.lm, as.data.frame(meuse.grid), interval = "prediction")
meuse.grid$fit = pr[, 1]
meuse.grid$upper = pr[, 3]
meuse.grid$lower = pr[, 2]
spplot(meuse.grid, c("fit", "lower", "upper"), sp.layout = meuse.lt, layout = c(3, 
    1))

lzn.lm = lm(log(zinc) ~ sqrt(dist), meuse.sf)
pr = predict(lzn.lm, as.data.frame(meuse.grid.stars), interval = "prediction")
meuse.grid.stars$fit = pr[, 1]
meuse.grid.stars$upper = pr[, 3]
meuse.grid.stars$lower = pr[, 2]
tm_shape(meuse.grid.stars) + 
  tm_raster(c("fit","lower","upper"),n=20,title = "prediction", palette = bpy.palette) + 
  tm_shape(meuse.riv.sf)+tm_polygons() + 
  tm_shape(meuse)+tm_symbols(shape = 3,size = 0.2,col="black") +
  tm_facets(ncol = 3,nrow=1,free.scales = FALSE) + 
  tm_layout(legend.outside.size = 0.15, panel.labels = c("tr1","tr2","tr3"))

Note that all these prediction intervals refer to prediction of single observations; confidence intervals for mean values (i.e., the regression line alone) are obtained by specifying interval = "confidence".

Exercise 5

Exercise 5

Compared to prediction intervals, the intervals obtained by interval = "confidence" are

  1. wider
  2. narrower
  3. of approximately equal width

Another regression model is obtained by relating zinc to flood frequency, try

  • sp
  • sf/stars
plot(log(zinc) ~ ffreq, meuse)

meuse$ffreq <- as.factor(meuse$ffreq)
plot(log(zinc) ~ ffreq, meuse)

plot(log(zinc) ~ ffreq, meuse.sf)

meuse.sf$ffreq <- as.factor(meuse.sf$ffreq)
plot(log(zinc) ~ ffreq, meuse.sf)

Exercise 6

Exercise 6

Why is a box-and whisker plot drawn for the second plot, and not a scatter plot?

  1. ffreq has been turned into a categorical variable with numeric levels
  2. ffreq has been turned into a variable with only three values, 1, 2 and 3
  3. flood frequency is always expressed by boxes

Exercise 7

Exercise 7

What do the boxes in a box-and whisker plot refer to?

  1. the mean plus and minus one standard deviation of the group
  2. the 95 percent confidence interval for the mean
  3. the inter-quartile range (from 25-th to 75-th percentiles)

Exercise 8

Exercise 8

What is needed for flood frequency to use it for mapping zinc?

  1. it needs to be the number of times a grid cell floods
  2. it needs to be known at each prediction location (i.e., grid cells in meuse.grid)
  3. it needs to be independent of distance to the river

Try the model in prediction mode:

  • sp
  • sf/stars
lzn.lm2 = lm(log(zinc) ~ ffreq, meuse)
summary(lzn.lm2)

Call:
lm(formula = log(zinc) ~ ffreq, data = meuse)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.43726 -0.51417 -0.04058  0.46446  1.30060 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  6.21638    0.06852  90.727  < 2e-16 ***
ffreq2      -0.76415    0.11362  -6.725 3.34e-10 ***
ffreq3      -0.63323    0.14778  -4.285 3.23e-05 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.628 on 152 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.2531,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.2433 
F-statistic: 25.75 on 2 and 152 DF,  p-value: 2.338e-10
pr = predict(lzn.lm2, meuse.grid, interval = "prediction")
meuse.grid$fit = pr[, 1]
meuse.grid$upper = pr[, 3]
meuse.grid$lower = pr[, 2]
spplot(meuse.grid, c("fit", "lower", "upper"), sp.layout = meuse.lt, layout = c(3,1))

lzn.lm2 = lm(log(zinc) ~ ffreq, meuse.sf)
summary(lzn.lm2)

Call:
lm(formula = log(zinc) ~ ffreq, data = meuse.sf)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.43726 -0.51417 -0.04058  0.46446  1.30060 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  6.21638    0.06852  90.727  < 2e-16 ***
ffreq2      -0.76415    0.11362  -6.725 3.34e-10 ***
ffreq3      -0.63323    0.14778  -4.285 3.23e-05 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.628 on 152 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.2531,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.2433 
F-statistic: 25.75 on 2 and 152 DF,  p-value: 2.338e-10
pr = predict(lzn.lm2, meuse.grid.stars, interval = "prediction")
meuse.grid.stars$fit = pr[, 1]
meuse.grid.stars$upper = pr[, 3]
meuse.grid.stars$lower = pr[, 2]
tm_shape(meuse.grid.stars) + 
  tm_raster(c("fit", "lower", "upper"), palette = bpy.palette, title = "", n=20) + 
  tm_shape(meuse.riv)+tm_polygons() + 
  tm_shape(meuse.sf) + 
  tm_symbols(size = 0.2,shape = 3,col = "black") + 
  tm_facets(free.scales = FALSE) +
  tm_layout(legend.outside.size = 0.15, panel.labels = c("fit","lower","upper"))

Exercise 9

Exercise 9

Why is the pattern obtained not smooth?

References

Burrough, P. A., and R. A. McDonnell. 1998. Principles of Geographical Information Systems. Oxford University Press.
Pebesma, Edzer. 2004. “Multivariable Geostatistics in S: The Gstat Package.” Computers & Geosciences 30: 683–91.
———. 2022a. Sf: Simple Features for r. https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=sf.
———. 2022b. Stars: Spatiotemporal Arrays, Raster and Vector Data Cubes. https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=stars.
Pebesma, Edzer, and Roger Bivand. 2022a. Sp: Classes and Methods for Spatial Data. https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=sp.
———. 2022b. Spatial Data Science, with Applications in R. online. https://r-spatial.org/book/.
Pebesma, Edzer, and Benedikt Graeler. 2022. Gstat: Spatial and Spatio-Temporal Geostatistical Modelling, Prediction and Simulation. https://github.com/r-spatial/gstat/.
Rikken, M. G. J., and RPG Van Rijn. 1993. Soil Pollution with Heavy Metals - an Inquiry into Spatial Variation, Cost of Mapping and the Risk Evaluation of Copper, Cadmium, Lead and Zinc in the Floodplains of the Meuse West of Stein, the Netherlands. Doctoraalveldwerkverslag. Dept. of Physical Geography, Utrecht University.
Tennekes, Martijn. 2022. Tmap: Thematic Maps. https://github.com/r-tmap/tmap.
